title: BloodHound — Comprehensive AD Graph Guide
body: Use this after your first manual AD pass. SharpHound collects AD relationships and BloodHound turns them into a graph so you can reason about paths instead of isolated facts. The workflow is: collect, import, mark owned principals, run path queries, then manually validate every important edge before acting.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# BLOODHOUND WORKFLOW — AD GRAPH ENUMERATION
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Authorized lab / assessment use only.
# Goal: collect AD relationships, import them into BloodHound,
# mark what you own, then find and validate attack paths.

# ── 0) BASELINE BEFORE AUTOMATION ─────────────────
whoami /all
hostname
ipconfig /all
net user /domain
net group "Domain Admins" /domain
net accounts /domain

# Do not start with a graph blindly. Know:
# - current user
# - current workstation
# - domain
# - DC / DNS server
# - password and lockout policy
# - scope

# ── 1) IMPORT SHARPHOUND ON WINDOWS FOOTHOLD ──────
cd C:\Users\$env:USERNAME\Downloads
powershell -ep bypass
Import-Module .\SharpHound.ps1

# Confirm command help and available collection methods.
Get-Help Invoke-BloodHound
Get-Help Invoke-BloodHound -Detailed

# ── 2) STANDARD COLLECTION SNAPSHOT ───────────────
Invoke-BloodHound `
  -CollectionMethod All `
  -OutputDirectory "$env:USERPROFILE\Desktop" `
  -OutputPrefix "corp-audit"

# Output should include a BloodHound .zip on the Desktop.
# Keep the .zip. The .bin cache can help repeated collection,
# but the .zip is what you import into BloodHound.

# ── 3) TARGETED COLLECTION WHEN ALL IS TOO LOUD ───
Invoke-BloodHound `
  -CollectionMethod Group,LocalAdmin,Session,LoggedOn,ACL,ObjectProps,SPNTargets,Trusts `
  -Throttle 1000 `
  -Jitter 20 `
  -OutputDirectory "$env:USERPROFILE\Desktop" `
  -OutputPrefix "corp-targeted"

# ── 4) LOOPING FOR SESSION CHANGES ────────────────
# Useful when logged-on users matter and users may log in later.
Invoke-BloodHound `
  -CollectionMethod Session,LoggedOn `
  -Loop `
  -LoopDuration 02:00:00 `
  -LoopInterval 00:10:00 `
  -OutputDirectory "$env:USERPROFILE\Desktop" `
  -OutputPrefix "corp-session-loop"

# ── 5) START NEO4J + BLOODHOUND ON KALI ───────────
sudo neo4j start

# Neo4j web UI:
# http://127.0.0.1:7474
# First login is commonly neo4j / neo4j, then set a new password.

bloodhound

# In BloodHound:
# Upload Data → select the SharpHound .zip.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# BLOODHOUND ANALYSIS CHECKLIST
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1. Open Database Info.
#    Check users, groups, computers, sessions, ACLs, and local admin edges.
#
# 2. Run built-in queries:
#    - Find all Domain Admins
#    - Find Shortest Paths to Domain Admins
#    - Find Shortest Paths to High Value Targets
#    - Find Computers where Domain Users are Local Admin
#    - Find Kerberoastable Users
#    - Find AS-REP Roastable Users
#    - Find Principals with DCSync Rights
#    - Find Dangerous Rights over Domain
#    - Find Workstations where Domain Admins have Sessions
#
# 3. Mark owned objects:
#    Search current user → right-click → Mark User as Owned
#    Search current workstation → right-click → Mark Computer as Owned
#
# 4. Re-run shortest-path queries from owned principals.
#
# 5. For every useful edge, ask:
#    - What object do I control?
#    - What object can I affect?
#    - What permission or relationship connects them?
#    - Can I validate it manually?
#    - Does it move me closer to DA, a server, credentials, or data?

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# EDGE READING — WHAT THE GRAPH IS TELLING YOU
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# MemberOf              = user/group is member of another group
# AdminTo               = principal has local admin on computer
# HasSession            = user has active session on computer
# CanRDP                = principal can RDP to computer
# CanPSRemote           = principal can PowerShell Remoting to computer
# GenericAll            = full control over object
# GenericWrite          = can edit useful object attributes
# WriteDacl             = can modify object permissions
# WriteOwner            = can take ownership
# AddMember             = can add users to group
# ForceChangePassword   = can reset target user's password
# AllExtendedRights     = broad extended rights on object
# DCSync                = can replicate domain secrets

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# ATTACK-PATH THINKING
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Pattern:
#   owned user -> AdminTo computer -> high-value user HasSession -> privilege path
#
# Example reasoning:
#   stephanie AdminTo CLIENT74
#   jeffadmin HasSession CLIENT74
#   jeffadmin MemberOf Domain Admins
#
# Meaning:
#   If stephanie can get admin execution on CLIENT74 and jeffadmin's
#   credential material is present there, the graph shows a possible
#   path toward domain admin. Validate before touching it.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# MANUAL VALIDATION COMMANDS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Validate local admin access.
Find-LocalAdminAccess

# Validate group membership.
net group "Domain Admins" /domain
Get-DomainGroupMember "Domain Admins" -Recurse

# Validate sessions.
Get-NetSession -ComputerName CLIENT74 -Verbose
.\PsLoggedon.exe \\CLIENT74

# Validate ACL edge.
Get-ObjectAcl -Identity "TARGET_OBJECT" -ResolveGUIDs |
  ? {$_.ActiveDirectoryRights -match "GenericAll|GenericWrite|WriteDacl|WriteOwner|AllExtendedRights|ForceChangePassword"} |
  select IdentityReferenceName,ActiveDirectoryRights,ObjectDN

# Validate SPN / Kerberoast edge.
Get-DomainUser -SPN | select samaccountname,serviceprincipalname

# Validate AS-REP roastable users.
Get-DomainUser -PreauthNotRequired | select samaccountname,useraccountcontrol

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# NOTES TO CAPTURE
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# - Collection method
# - Timestamp
# - User context used for collection
# - Owned principals marked
# - Exact BloodHound path screenshot
# - Edge names in the path
# - Manual validation command/output
# - Why the path matters
# - Next action
warn: SharpHound can create a noticeable spike in LDAP, SMB, session, and registry traffic. In labs and OSCP practice this is useful. In real engagements, coordinate scope and timing. Never treat a BloodHound path as proof by itself; validate the edge manually first.

choices.label: Need PowerView commands to validate the graph
